questions.source: IGotAnOffer; SRE Book Shakespeare example; SRE-INTERVIEW-CASES W-I16
questions.question: You are on-call for the Shakespeare search service. A black-box probe alert fires: search for "the forms of things unknown" has failed for five minutes. Synthetic checks are red. What should you do FIRST?
options: Confirm user impact (real traffic vs probe-only), check recent deploys/changes, and triage search pipeline health before deep debugging
Immediately restart every server in every region without checking dashboards
Ignore the alert because literary quotes are not real queries
Wait 24 hours to see if the probe self-heals
questions.explanation: IGotAnOffer and SRE troubleshooting guidance: start with impact scope, change correlation, and service-level signals — then narrow to indexing, serving, or dependency failure.

questions.question: Dashboards show green synthetic probes and "clean" error logs, but support tickets report widespread user failures. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Synthetic probes or log sampling miss the failing path — real user traffic hits a different code path, region, or cohort
Users are lying; the system is definitely healthy
Green dashboards prove all regions and features work
Error logs always capture 100% of user-visible failures
questions.explanation: A classic unexpected troubleshooting trap: probes test a narrow slice. Interviewers want you to compare synthetic vs real traffic, canary coverage, and client-side failures.

questions.source: Glassdoor SRE progressive Apache debug; SRE-INTERVIEW-CASES W-I12
questions.question: Live-debug interview: a simple website returns permission errors for a static file. Apache logs show 403. The file exists on disk as a blank text file. What is the most likely fix?
options: File ownership or mode is too restrictive for the Apache user — adjust permissions so the web server can read it
Rename the file to .php so Apache executes it
Delete Apache and use Python only
The 403 always means DNS failure
questions.explanation: Glassdoor's progressive Apache gauntlet starts with a basic permissions problem. Read logs, verify path, check owner/mode — classic page-1 trap.

questions.source: Glassdoor SRE Apache debug page 2; SRE-INTERVIEW-CASES W-I13
questions.question: Next live-debug page: logs show 404 for `challenge2.shtml` but `ls` in the document root shows no such file. What should you check NEXT?
options: Apache configuration — the URL may map to a different directory than the one you listed
Reinstall the Linux kernel
Assume the interviewer typo'd the filename and stop
Only check browser cache; never read server config
questions.explanation: Page 2 of the Glassdoor escape-room: the file lives elsewhere per Apache config. Candidates who only `ls` the obvious webroot get stuck.

questions.source: Glassdoor SRE Apache debug page 3; SRE-INTERVIEW-CASES W-I14
questions.question: Live-debug: a filename looks correct in the terminal (`challenge3.shtml`) but Apache logs show URL-encoded characters and 500 errors. `ls` shows a matching name. What is happening?
options: Unicode homoglyphs — the on-disk name uses look-alike characters; search for the literal ASCII string to find the wrong file
Apache cannot serve .shtml files by design
The terminal is lying about file existence
500 errors always mean out-of-memory
questions.explanation: Glassdoor page 3: Unicode filenames render like ASCII in the terminal. Open/search for the exact expected string — a memorable "weird" SRE debug moment.

questions.source: Glassdoor SRE Apache debug page 4; SRE-INTERVIEW-CASES W-I15
questions.question: Final live-debug page: requesting `challenge4.shtml` segfaults Apache. `gdb` on the core dump points to mod_rewrite C source line with `strstr(filename, "challenge4.shtml")` followed by `SIGSEGV`. What do you tell the interviewer?
options: The module deliberately segfaults when that filename matches — explain the strstr check triggers intentional SIGSEGV; fixing is not required
strstr always causes segfaults in Apache
You must patch the kernel to allow segfaults
gdb output is unreliable for Apache modules
questions.explanation: Glassdoor page 4: candidates run gdb, read the C source, and explain intentional crash on a specific filename. They are not asked to fix it — only diagnose.

questions.source: Exponent L6 EM fd33b1; SRE-INTERVIEW-CASES W-M09
questions.question: Exponent reports failing Google's must-pass EM code review round (200+ line Google Doc). What re-interview constraint do some candidates report?
options: A long cooldown (often ~12 months) before re-interviewing for the same level after failing a must-pass round
Automatic promotion to the next level on retry
No cooldown — you can retry the next day with the same packet
Failed code review rounds are never scored
questions.explanation: Exponent L6 EM failure stories note harsh cooldown on must-pass code review — especially painful when time was lost reading legacy logic, not missing fundamentals.

questions.question: A Reddit candidate reports only three onsite interviews after phone screens, while blog posts describe four to five. Best takeaway?
options: Loop length and round mix vary by level, track, and team — confirm your schedule with the recruiter
Every Google SRE loop is identical worldwide
Three rounds means the candidate was down-leveled automatically
Blog posts are always authoritative over recruiters
questions.explanation: 1kdf6fn is a real variance data point. Prep for your confirmed rounds, not generic blog templates.

questions.question: reddit:da4h29: recruiter prep covered mutex, semaphore, and filesystem basics and felt "too easy." Onsite still felt harder. What explains the gap?
options: Prep topics appear inside different round shapes (code review snippets, NALSD math, troubleshooting) — not as standalone trivia questions
Recruiter prep is always identical to onsite question wording
Easy prep means onsite will only ask behavioral questions
Candidates should ignore recruiter prep entirely
questions.explanation: da4h29 pattern: fundamentals show up embedded in production-style review and design — the format surprise matters as much as the topic list.

questions.source: SRE Workbook NALSD; DEV walkthrough; SRE-INTERVIEW-CASES W-I06
questions.question: Many candidates treat Google's NALSD round like "design Twitter from scratch." What is NALSD actually?
options: Analyze and incrementally improve an existing large production system under real constraints — not greenfield boxes
Only draw microservices with no numbers
A LeetCode hard dynamic programming round
A behavioral interview about favorite programming languages
questions.explanation: NALSD = Non-Abstract Large System Design. The "weird" surprise is fixing/diagnosing a broken system with napkin math — c3lrks and workbook materials align.

questions.question: IGotAnOffer aggregates "Design a snakes game" alongside AdWords-scale NALSD prompts. How should you respond if you get a toy-sounding design prompt?
options: Treat it seriously — clarify requirements, estimate scale, state SLOs, and iterate; toy prompts still test NALSD process
Refuse because games are not serious engineering
Only discuss graphics APIs and skip reliability
Assume it is a trick and end the interview
questions.explanation: Prompt surface area varies; interviewers still score requirements, math, failure modes, and incremental design — even for game-like scenarios.

questions.source: Jointaro SRE Manager US; SRE-INTERVIEW-CASES W-M05
questions.question: Jointaro reports a Google SRE Manager system-design prompt: "Design a system to understand trends in a non-structured dataset." What should you do FIRST?
options: Clarify data volume, sources, latency needs, who consumes trends, privacy, and what "trend" means operationally
Immediately pick Hadoop because big data
Draw Kubernetes clusters without asking questions
Decline because the prompt is too vague to discuss
questions.explanation: Vague product-ish prompts are intentional. Manager-track design still rewards clarifiers before architecture — same as Systems Architecture prep.

questions.question: Glassdoor: distribute 1 TB from one server to 10,000 nodes; copying to one node takes hours. How do you avoid ~20,000 hours total serial copy time?
options: Use tree/broadcast fanout, P2P-style propagation, or staged distribution so nodes help replicate — plus checksums for corruption detection
Copy serially to each node and accept 20,000 hours
Email the terabyte to each administrator
Compression eliminates the need for any network transfer
questions.explanation: Classic fanout math trap: O(n) serial copies explode. Interviewers want layered distribution and integrity checks — reported on Glassdoor SRE interviews.

questions.question: Glassdoor SRE interview: "What's 2^32?" What approach best demonstrates systems thinking?
options: Decompose: 2^32 = 2^10 × 2^10 × 2^10 × 2^2 ≈ 1000³ × 4 ≈ 4 billion; note unsigned 32-bit overflow boundary
Memorize exactly 4,294,967,296 with no reasoning
Answer "4 giga" and stop
Say it is impossible to estimate powers of two
questions.explanation: Glassdoor reports mental-math decomposition, not calculator precision. Connecting to uint32 max is a strong SRE signal.

questions.question: Glassdoor reports: "Code the tail command for large datasets." Which approach is correct?
options: Seek near end of file and read backwards in chunks — do not load the entire file into memory
Read all lines into a Python list with readlines()
Copy the file to RAM disk first
tail requires loading the full file to count newlines from the start
questions.explanation: Even if your loop is code review, IC-track reports include practical Unix-style tasks. Bounded memory via seek/chunked read is the expected insight.

questions.question: Glassdoor: find the most repeated words in a large text file. What is the core scalable approach?
options: Stream the file once, count with a hash map / Counter, track max — O(n) time, bounded by unique words not file reread
Sort the entire file on disk then scan once
Load the file into a string and split without streaming
Ask the user to manually count
questions.explanation: Practical file-processing question — tests streaming and aggregation, not graph algorithms.

questions.question: IGotAnOffer / Glassdoor: "What is a zombie process?" What is the accurate answer?
options: A child process that exited but whose parent has not called wait() — still has a PID entry until reaped
Any process using 100% CPU
A process infected with malware
A thread inside the kernel only
questions.explanation: Phone-screen "basic" Linux that stumps ops folks. Zombies are about parent/child lifecycle, not CPU usage.

questions.question: What's the difference between a hard link and a soft (symbolic) link in Linux?
options: Hard link shares the same inode; symlink is a separate path pointing to another path — symlinks can cross filesystems, hard links typically cannot
Hard links only work on directories; symlinks only work on files
They are identical; ln always creates symlinks
Symlinks increase inode count; hard links do not reference inodes
questions.explanation: Foundational but still appears in senior SRE phone screens per IGotAnOffer aggregation.

questions.question: IGotAnOffer lists: "Which system call's return value is not zero although it ended successfully?" Which family of answers is correct?
options: Calls like wait()/waitpid() return the child PID (positive), or lseek() may return a non-zero offset — success ≠ return 0
All successful syscalls always return exactly 0
Only failed syscalls return non-zero values
read() always returns 0 on success
questions.explanation: Classic trick question — tests whether you know success is not always zero (e.g., wait returns pid, read returns byte count).

questions.question: When asked "What happens when you type `ls -l`?" in a Linux internals interview, what depth is expected FIRST?
options: Shell parses input → fork/exec → ls binary → getdents/stat syscalls → format output — high-level path before struct trivia
Recite every field in task_struct from memory
Answer only "it lists files" with no further detail
Explain GPU driver internals
questions.explanation: wc4ryg debate: implications and call chain beat memorizing kernel structs. Walk shell → fork → exec → filesystem syscalls.

questions.question: IGotAnOffer: "What's the difference between TCP and UDP?" Which answer fits SRE interviews?
options: TCP: connection-oriented, reliable, ordered delivery; UDP: connectionless, no delivery guarantees — choose based on latency/tolerance for loss
TCP is always faster than UDP for all workloads
UDP is only used for DNS and never elsewhere
They are identical above layer 3
questions.explanation: Basic but frequently listed for Google SRE phone screens — tie to reliability vs latency trade-offs.

questions.question: Latency spikes immediately after a deploy; error rate is rising. Dashboards show CPU is normal. What should you investigate FIRST?
options: Correlate deploy timeline, check canary vs full rollout, and compare golden signals / traces for the new binary vs rollback candidate
Assume users are imagining latency
Wait a week for metrics to stabilize
Only check CPU because it is the only signal that matters
questions.explanation: Post-deploy regression is a common unexpected troubleshooting narrative — change correlation before deep kernel dives.

questions.question: reddit:raabnp asks when to walk away from an SRE interview process. Which situation best justifies declining to continue?
options: Job posting emphasized troubleshooting/ops but onsite is only LeetCode with no connection to stated role — misaligned process
Interviewer asks a clarifying question you do not know
You must use a Google Doc instead of an IDE
NALSD includes napkin math
questions.explanation: raabnp philosophy: severe role/process mismatch is a walk-away trigger — not normal Google format quirks like Docs or NALSD math.

questions.source: Jointaro Google EM MV; SRE-INTERVIEW-CASES W-M14
questions.question: Jointaro Google EM reports deadlock vs livelock in a system design discussion. What is the key distinction?
options: Deadlock: threads blocked forever waiting on each other; livelock: threads keep changing state but make no progress
They are identical terms for mutex contention
Livelock only happens in databases; deadlock only in kernels
Both mean the process exited with code 0
questions.explanation: Classic concurrency topic appearing in manager design rounds — unexpected if you only prepped people-management stories.

questions.question: Exponent EM candidates report code review in a plain Google Doc with no IDE, syntax highlighting, or run button. Best adaptation?
options: Manual trace with small test inputs, triage correctness before style, and narrate reasoning aloud
Refuse to review without an IDE
Spend most time fixing variable naming
Assume typos cannot exist because Google Docs has spellcheck
questions.explanation: Format surprise is real — muscle memory from IDE debugging does not transfer. Process matches CR-022/026 prep.

questions.question: reddit:1fm8wmh was surprised by heavy LeetCode (graphs/DP) when expecting practical SRE coding. Best explanation?
options: SRE-SWE track can include algorithmic coding; SRE-SE leans practical/Linux — confirm your track with recruiter
All Google SRE loops never include algorithms
LeetCode only appears for managers
Surprise means the interview was invalid
questions.explanation: Track variance is the weird part — 1fm8wmh vs 1klu37j show different bars. Your confirmed loop is code review, but know IC variance.

questions.question: Glassdoor (Booking.com SRE): given hotel tuples `(id, name, city)`, return cities with ≥3 hotels sharing the same name. Core approach?
options: Count (city, hotel_name) pairs with a hash map; emit city when any name reaches count 3
Sort all tuples by hotel_id and binary search
Return every city in the input
Only check the first hotel name in each city
questions.explanation: Product-flavored coding reported on Glassdoor — hash/count pattern, not graph DP.

questions.source: DEV NALSD math traps; B5-013; SRE-INTERVIEW-CASES §3.4
questions.question: In NALSD you must replicate 5 PB cross-region with 4-hour RTO over a 10 Gbps link. Quick physics check: can this work?
options: No — 5 PB over 10 Gbps takes on the order of weeks; flag impossibility and discuss revised RTO, more bandwidth, or partial replication
Yes — 10 Gbps easily moves 5 PB in 4 hours
Yes — compress 5 PB to 1 MB with gzip
Skip math — cloud is infinite
questions.explanation: Stopping when math fails is a pass signal in NALSD. Already in B5-013; WX reinforces the weird "say no" moment.
